note: Mathlib/MeasureTheory/Covering/Differentiation.lean:867 — 'Lebesgue differentiation theorem: for a.e. x, average of ‖f y - f x‖ on a tends to 0 as a shrinks to x along a Vitali family.' This is exactly the a.e.-every-point-is-a-Lebesgue-point statement, but generalized to abstract Vitali families/metric measure spaces rather than a named 'Lebesgue point' predicate on ℝⁿ.
verify_note: Decl exists (Mathlib.MeasureTheory.Covering.Differentiation, confirmed via decl_exists + source read at line 867); docstring literally reads 'Lebesgue differentiation theorem: for almost every point x, the average of ‖f y - f x‖ on a tends to 0 as a shrinks to x along a Vitali family' — the exact classical Lebesgue-point condition, stated abstractly. Grepped mathlib4 for any named IsLebesguePoint/LebesguePoint predicate — none exists, so there is no more-direct decl and 'generalization' (not 'exact') is the honest, non-overclaiming match_kind; Q388660 ('Lebesgue point': type of point where a function does not oscillate too much) matches the theorem's content. Confidence medium is appropriately conservative.
